era_context: The late 15th century continued to see the consolidation of European nation-states, the flourishing of the Italian Renaissance, and the expansion of the Ottoman Empire. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty maintained control of China, while Japan remained fragmented. The Age of Exploration was on the horizon but had not yet begun. This was a period of relative stability in Europe following the Wars of the Roses in England and the Reconquista in Iberia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited European documentation; indigenous records sparse)
Interior Americas (no European contact; archaeological record incomplete)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited European sources; Islamic and Chinese records exist but underrepresented)

events.title: Treaty of Picquigny
events.description: The Treaty of Picquigny, signed in August 1475, formally ended the Hundred Years' War between England and France. English King Edward IV agreed to withdraw his forces from France in exchange for a pension and the promise of a marriage alliance. The treaty marked the effective end of English territorial ambitions in France and consolidated French power under Louis XI. The agreement was brokered by the Duke of Burgundy and represented a shift in the balance of power in Western Europe. The treaty is often cited as the symbolic end of the medieval period in Franco-English relations.
key_figures: Charles the Bold, Duke of Burgundy
events.certainty_note: The treaty is well-documented in contemporary sources and the date is secure.
cross_references: 1453-001 (Fall of Constantinople, broader context of European consolidation)

events.title: Michelangelo Begins Apprenticeship
events.description: Michelangelo Buonarroti (born 1475) began his apprenticeship with the sculptor Donatello and later with the painter Ghirlandaio in Florence. While Michelangelo was born in 1475, his formal apprenticeship began around 1488–1489. However, 1475 marks his birth and the beginning of the life that would define the High Renaissance. Michelangelo's work—including the David (1504) and the Sistine Chapel ceiling (1508–1512)—would become iconic representations of Renaissance art and human achievement. His birth in 1475 is significant as a marker of the cultural flourishing of the Italian Renaissance.

sources.name: Vasari's Lives of the Artists (written 16th century)

events.certainty_note: Michelangelo's birth date (March 6, 1475) is well-documented. His apprenticeship began later, around 1488–1489.

events.title: Reign of Sixtus IV Continues
events.description: Pope Sixtus IV (r. 1471–1484) continued his reign in 1475, a period marked by significant patronage of the arts and aggressive political expansion of the Papal States. Sixtus commissioned the Sistine Chapel (begun 1477) and engaged in nepotism and military campaigns to expand papal territorial control. His reign was controversial: he was known for his support of the Pazzi Conspiracy against the Medici and his involvement in various political intrigues. Sixtus's papacy represents the intersection of Renaissance patronage and medieval papal politics, and his actions had significant consequences for Italian politics and the future of the Catholic Church.
key_figures: Giuliano della Rovere (future Pope Julius II)
events.certainty_note: Sixtus IV's reign is well-documented in papal records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration of the African Coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator (who died in 1460, but whose legacy continued), continued systematic exploration of the West African coast in 1475. The Portuguese had established trading posts and forts along the coast, including São Jorge da Mina (Elmina Castle), which was constructed in 1482 but planning and earlier expeditions occurred in 1475. Portuguese exploration was driven by the desire for gold, spices, and a sea route to Asia. The expeditions were methodical and well-documented, and they laid the groundwork for the Age of Exploration. By 1475, the Portuguese had reached the equator and were preparing for further expansion.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (deceased 1460, legacy continued)
events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration in 1475 is attested in royal records and later chronicles. The specific achievements of 1475 are less well-documented than later voyages, but the general trajectory is clear.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: Wars of the Roses: Battle of Tewkesbury (1471) Aftermath
events.description: In 1475, England was in a period of relative stability following the Battle of Tewkesbury (1471), which secured Edward IV's position as king. The Wars of the Roses (1455–1487) were a series of civil wars between the houses of York and Lancaster. By 1475, Edward IV had consolidated his power, though the conflict would resume after his death in 1483. The period 1475–1483 represents a relative peace in England, allowing for economic recovery and cultural development. The stability was fragile, however, and would collapse into the conflict that brought Richard III to power and eventually led to the Tudor victory at Bosworth Field (1485).
key_figures: Margaret of Anjou (Lancastrian claimant, in exile)
Richard, Duke of Gloucester (future Richard III)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on the Wars of the Roses

events.certainty_note: The political situation in England in 1475 is well-documented in contemporary sources.
cross_references: 1483-001 (Edward IV's death and Richard III's rise)

events.title: Ming Dynasty Consolidation Under Zhu Youanzhang
events.description: The Ming Dynasty, under the Chenghua Emperor (r. 1464–1487), continued its consolidation of power in 1475. The Chenghua Emperor's reign was marked by the influence of eunuchs and the continuation of the Great Wall construction. The Ming maintained a strong centralized bureaucracy and a powerful military. In 1475, the Ming were at the height of their power, controlling a unified China and maintaining tributary relationships with neighboring states. The period represents the stability and cultural flourishing of the Ming Dynasty, which would continue until the Qing conquest in 1644.
key_figures: Eunuch officials (particularly influential during this reign)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on the Ming Dynasty

events.certainty_note: The Ming Dynasty's consolidation and stability in 1475 are well-documented in official records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Picquigny is sometimes presented as a definitive end to the Hundred Years' War, but modern historians note that the conflict had already been effectively concluded by the fall of Bordeaux in 1453. The treaty was more a formalization of an existing reality than a dramatic turning point. Additionally, the characterization of 1475 as a period of 'stability' in England is somewhat misleading; the Wars of the Roses were merely in remission, and the underlying tensions that would erupt in 1483 were already present. The notion that Michelangelo's birth in 1475 marks the 'beginning' of the High Renaissance is a retrospective construction; the Renaissance was already well underway, and Michelangelo's significance was not apparent until decades later.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1475 is rich in Western European sources, particularly for political and military events. However, the sources are heavily weighted toward court chronicles, papal records, and later compilations. The perspective is predominantly that of political and military elites. Indigenous perspectives from the Americas, Africa, and Asia are absent from surviving European sources. Ottoman records exist but are underrepresented in Western historiography. The year is well-documented for political and military events but less so for economic, social, and cultural developments outside elite circles. Chinese sources are abundant but often underrepresented in Western historical syntheses.

graph_edges.note: The Treaty of Picquigny formalized the end of the Hundred Years' War, which had begun in 1337 and was effectively concluded by 1453.
The relative stability of 1475 under Edward IV was disrupted by his death in 1483, leading to Richard III's rise.
Michelangelo's birth in 1475 led to his creation of the David in 1504.
